pi_items.source: Official OpenBCI documentation (docs.openbci.com) confirms default recording directory is [USER]/Documents/OpenBCI_GUI/Recordings. Verified in setup_openbci.sh line 16.
Task description states 'Allow the recording to run for AT LEAST 10 seconds'. Standard OpenBCI recording duration for basic tasks.

At 250 Hz sample rate, a 10-second recording yields 2500 data rows (plus ~5 header lines). The threshold of 2000 is approximately 8 seconds of data, which is below the stated 10-second minimum duration.

OpenBCI documentation indicates ~1 megabyte per minute for 8-channel 250Hz recordings. A 10-second recording produces approximately 167 KB (10/60 * 1000 KB). The metadata minimum of 50 KB is significantly below the actual expected file size. Verified through web search results from OpenBCI forum and documentation.

Official OpenBCI Cyton specifications confirm 250 Hz as the standard sample rate. Data is retrieved from ADS1299 ICs at 250 samples per second. Verified in Cyton Data Format and Cyton Specs documentation.
Official OpenBCI Cyton Board is an 8-channel bio-amplifier. Verified in product documentation (shop.openbci.com) and technical specifications.

Task description explicitly states: 'Select SYNTHETIC (algorithmic) as the Data Source'. OpenBCI GUI documentation confirms this synthetic data source exists for testing without hardware. Implementation uses BoardBrainFlowSynthetic class in OpenBCI GUI code.

privileged_info_summary: The task records synthetic EEG data using OpenBCI GUI v5.2.2 (August 2023 release) with the SYNTHETIC (algorithmic) data source. Data is stored in /home/ga/Documents/OpenBCI_GUI/Recordings directory. OpenBCI Cyton specifications confirm 8-channel configuration at 250 Hz sample rate. A 10-second recording produces approximately 2500 data rows and ~167 KB file size. The metadata threshold of 50 KB minimum file size is inconsistent with actual OpenBCI data generation rates (~1 MB/minute), which would produce approximately 167 KB for 10 seconds of recording. The min_data_rows threshold of 2000 corresponds to only 8 seconds at 250 Hz, below the stated 10-second minimum recording duration.
